title: The Great War of the Soul: Divine and Human Madness in Jung's Liber Novus
author: Unknown

body.text:
International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Applications(ICAIA)
https://worldsiencepub.com/index.php/lmc June,2026 THE PYRAMID AND THE IMPORTANCE OF USING INTERACTIVE
TEACHING METHODS IN ITS INSTRUCTION
Makhmudov Azam Kudratovich
Teacher of mathematics at Terdu Academic Lyceum
Abstract. This article examines the conceptof the pyramid in mathematics and highlights
the importance of using interactive teaching methods during its instruction. Apyramid is athreedimensional geometric figure consisting of apolygonal base and triangular faces meeting at a
common vertex. Teaching this topic through interactive approaches increases student
engagement, improves spatial imagination, and strengthens problem-solving skills. Methods
such as collaborative learning, brainstorming, concept mapping, educational games, and digital
visualization help learners understand geometric relationships more effectively. Interactive
instruction transforms students from passive listeners into active participants, encouraging
critical thinking and communication.
Keywords: pyramid, geometry, mathematics education, interactive methods, collaborative
learning, visualization, spatial thinking, problem solving, educational technology, student
engagement.
Figure 1.
Simple Pyramid Illustration

Geometry plays afundamental role in mathematics education because it develops logical
reasoning, visualization, and analytical thinking. Among three-dimensional geometric figures,
the pyramid occupies an important place due to its practical applications in architecture,
engineering, and design. Apyramid is apolyhedron with apolygonal base and triangular lateral
faces that meet at asingle point called the apex. Understanding the structure of apyramid
enables students to connect abstract mathematical concepts with real-world objects.
Traditional teaching approaches often rely on lectures and memorization. Although these
methods may provide theoretical knowledge, they donot always encourage active participation.
Interactive teaching methods offer an effective alternative by engaging students directly in the
learning process. Through discussion, group work, digital tools, and hands-on activities, learners
become active constructors of knowledge rather than passive recipients.
One of the major advantages of interactive instruction is the developmentof spatial
imagination. Many students find it difficult to visualize three-dimensional figures from twodimensional drawings. Interactive models, paper constructions, and computer simulations allow
learners to manipulate geometric objects and observe their properties from different perspectives.
This process significantly improves conceptual understanding.
The study of pyramids provides an excellentopportunity to apply collaborative learning.
Students can work in groups to construct pyramid models using paper, cardboard, or digital Page 32 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Applications(ICAIA)
https://worldsiencepub.com/index.php/lmc June,2026

software. During these activities, they discuss geometric properties, compare results, and solve
problems collectively. Such collaboration strengthens communication skills and encourages peer
learning.
The brainstorming method can also be used effectively. At the beginning of alesson, the
teacher may ask students where pyramids can be found in everyday life. Learners may mention
ancient monuments, roofs of buildings, or decorative structures. This activity activates prior
knowledge and creates meaningful connections between mathematics and reality.
Educational games represent another interactive strategy. Teachers can organize
competitions in which students identify different types of pyramids, calculate measurements, or
solve geometry puzzles. These activities increase motivation and make learning enjoyable. As a
result, students are more willing to participate and retain information.
Digital technologies have transformed mathematics education. Interactive whiteboards,
dynamic geometry software, and online learning platforms provide powerful visualization
opportunities. Students can rotate virtual pyramids, measure dimensions, and observe changes in
real time. Such experiences help bridge the gap between theory and practice.
The mathematical formulas related to pyramids are central to the topic. The volume of a
pyramid is calculated using:
V = (1/3)Bh
where Vrepresents volume, Bis the area of the base, and his the perpendicular height.
The total surface area of aregular pyramid is:
S = B + (1/2)Pl
where Sis the total surface area, Bis the base area, Pis the perimeter of the base, and lis
the slant height.
Instead of simply memorizing these formulas, students should discover them through
investigation. For example, ateacher can demonstrate that three identical pyramids can fill a
prism with the same base area and height. This visual experiment helps learners understand why
the coefficientone-third appears in the volume formula.
Concept mapping is another valuable interactive method. Students create diagrams showing
relationships among concepts such as base, apex, height, lateral face, edge, volume, and surface
area. These visual representations help organize knowledge and improve long-term retention.
Project-based learning can further enrich instruction. Learners may investigate famous
pyramids around the world and analyze their geometric properties. Such projects integrate
mathematics with history, architecture, and cultural studies. Interdisciplinary learning enhances
relevance and broadens students’ perspectives.
Assessment should also reflect interactive principles. Rather than relying solely on written
examinations, teachers can evaluate presentations, group projects, model construction, and
classroom participation. Multiple forms of assessment provide amore comprehensive picture of
student achievement.
Research in mathematics education consistently shows that active learning improves
academic performance. Students who participate in interactive activities demonstrate greater
confidence, deeper understanding, and stronger problem-solving abilities. They are more likely
to apply mathematical knowledge in new situations and to develop positive attitudes toward the
subject.
In conclusion, the pyramid is an important geometric figure thatoffers rich opportunities for
mathematical exploration. Interactive teaching methods significantly enhance the learning
process by promoting engagement, collaboration, visualization, and critical thinking. When
combined with appropriate technology and hands-on experiences, these methods help students
understand both the theoretical and practical aspects of pyramid geometry. Therefore, educators
should integrate interactive strategies into mathematics instruction to improve learning outcomes
and foster meaningful understanding.
